title: Can Language Models Teach? Teacher Explanations Improve Student Performance via Personalization
abstract: A hallmark property of explainable AI models is the ability to teach other agents, communicating knowledge of how to perform a task. While Large Language Models (LLMs) perform complex reasoning by generating explanations for their predictions, it is unclear whether they also make good teachers for weaker agents. To address this, we consider a student-teacher framework between two LLM agents and study if, when, and how the teacher should intervene with natural language explanations to improve the student’s performance. Since communication is expensive, we define a budget such that the teacher only communicates explanations for a fraction of the data, after which the student should perform well on its own. We decompose the teaching problem along four axes: (1) if teacher’s test time in- tervention improve student predictions, (2) when it is worth explaining a data point, (3) how the teacher should personalize explanations to better teach the student, and (4) if teacher explanations also improve student performance on future unexplained data. We first show that teacher LLMs can indeed intervene on student reasoning to improve their performance. Next, inspired by the Theory of Mind abilities of effective teachers, we propose building two few-shot mental models of the student. The first model defines an Intervention Function that simulates the utility of an intervention, allowing the teacher to intervene when this utility is the highest and improving student performance at lower budgets. The second model enables the teacher to personalize explanations for a particular student and outperform unpersonalized teachers. We also demonstrate that in multi-turn interactions, teacher explanations generalize and learning from explained data improves student performance on future unexplained data. Finally, we also verify that misaligned teachers can lower student performance to random chance by intentionally misleading them.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This work centers around the research question: *can stronger LLMs teach weaker ones with their explanations? And if yes, when and how should the teacher do this*? It answers a few research questions, progressively building up from using a teacher LLM to generate explanations for a randomly sampled subset of the test data called *the intervention budget* (from 0 to 100% in steps of 20%), to also using the teacher LLM to select which points to generate explanations for, to additionally "personalise" the explanations by adding a few demonstrations of the student's behaviour to the prompt, and finally looking into whether the student can generalise from explanations on other examples to different test examples.   
The results are positive, showing that in some cases a stronger teacher can teach a weaker model and increase performance by a couple percentage points. The authors propose an interesting notion of expected utility that is meant to estimate comparatively which test example it should generate explanations for to gain the most performance boost. The most compelling result from the paper is that using this estimated expected utility, which is only based on a few demonstrations, the teacher can improve the student much more than when it generates explanation for a random subset of the test examples.

STRENGTHS:
The experimental setup mostly seems, as far as I can judge, sound and comprehensive (few-shot demonstrations used for models, 3 different seeds sampling from the models are used, 3 different reasoning datasets are used and two different family of LLMs).

As mentioned in the summary, I find the most compelling and interesting result and contribution from the paper the expected utility that can choose which test examples to generate an explanation for from only a few demonstrations.

WEAKNESSES:
1. I think the *main weakness* of this paper is how many details are left out of the paper. Based on the main text, I have many question and without the answer it's sometimes hard to judge the actual soundness of the setup and the conclusions derived from the experiments. See Questions section below. I want to stress that I think because of this weakness, I might have misunderstood the weaknesses I mention below, and they might turn out not to be weaknesses. However, because the presentation is such that this is all unclear to me even after looking at the full appendix, I feel like this needs to be changed to be able to judge the merits of this work.

2. Another weakness is that the performance increases seem very marginal. Often, the confidence intervals based on running with 3 seeds seem to overlap between no intervention and 100% intervention for RQ1, and from the appendix it seems that for LLama in fact generating explanations does not significantly help at all for some of the datasets (Figure 2 in the main paper, Figure 3 in appendix). For RQ2, you indeed see much larger improvements, but for those the teacher has access to a gold label, which I address below in point 3. Then when there's no access to gold labels (Figure 4b main text, table 8 appendix), the improvements from using the expected utility seem marginal / mostly overlapping with baseline confidence intervals. Additionally, I'm not sure I can find the results for LLama without access to gold labels? For RQ3, the ToM results from Figure 5 again seem to have overlapping confidence intervals with the unpersonalised CoT baseline. For RQ4, if I understand correctly, it seems to show that without access to gold labels for all explanations, using only a few, students can generalise performance across data points, although the numbers are hard to follow from the text (line 360-361 says two in-context examples improve student accuracy by 4% to 61% in round 1, is that without teaching? or after round 1?). But are there also results of baselines in RQ4, e.g. using unpersonalised CoT baseline?

3. The claim for RQ3 is that the model's Theory of Mind improves the explanations, because it conditions on human helpful explanations that flip the answer, and student's unhelpful explanations; but it seems like there is no baseline that only conditions on the human's helpful explanations; how can you then claim that it's theory of mind that's doing the work and not simply the human unpersonalised helpful explanations? Additionally, to claim that the model's theory of mind skills help improve explanations here is a bit strong, because I have seen no paper claim that LLama or Flan-T5 has theory of mind. The papers you refer to (5 and 43) only show that RLHF-ed models have ToM skills and LLama and Flan-T5 are not RLHF-ed. Further, other papers have shown that [5] doesn't use a valid experimental protocol (https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.08399) and yet others have shown that even from the RLHF-ed models, the ToM skills are not robust (work by Maarten Sap et al, e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14763).

3. Another weakness is that the limitations section is entirely written in the appendix, and it doesn't talk about the fact that the teacher model has to have access to the gold label for explanations; this seems to me like the most significant limitation of this method. It's hard to imagine what kind of applications you can use this method if you always need the ground-truth label for examples to get the student to improve.

QUESTIONS:
**In summary**, I think I can see the merit of this method when the teacher does not use gold labels for each explanation, but it's hard to judge from the current presentation of the results whether that does indeed lead to a better performance than baselines across datasets and models.

Below I'll ask questions surrounding my three points of weaknesses mentioned above.

1. Many details left out, making it hard to judge the soundness
- How do you know that the explanations of the teacher model never contain the ground-truth answer? Do you check this manually, or some other way?
- You say that the student model is weaker than the teacher; how do you measure this or is it a (fair) assumption based on the model size? I would explicitly state that.
- Why do you put all results for LLama in the appendix when you do mention it like it's part of the main evaluations in the experimental setup section?
- Most important question: what does it mean for the teacher to predict the confidence of the student on an example pre- and post-explanation? Do you ask the language model to output a float / number between 0-100? Or do you make predictions with the teacher as if it were the student? Right now, it's unclear from the main text.
- In general; what do you use as a confidence for the baselines in RQ2? Is it based on the likelihood the model assigns to the answer?
- Which model generates the CoT explanations for the baseline in RQ3?

2 & 3. performance increase marginal, and some claims seem not properly backed by ablations / baselines.  
Are the differences in performance between your method and the baselines significant? Can you summarise in the main text how much of an improvement your method usually gives, e.g. average improvement on the 3 datasets and for both model families? Can you show that the performance increase in RQ3 is really due to a combination of human helpful explanation and model unhelpful explanation and not simply because of the human helpful explanation?

4. Can you talk more in the main text about what this method could be helpful for given that it relies on availability to both a larger model and the ground-truth answer?
Or alternatively, can the experiments showing that it also works without ground-truth answer get more space in the main text, and also be done for all datasets and model families?

LIMITATIONS:
I would like to see more discussion in the main text of the limitation surrounding the need for (1) a larger model available for explanations at inference-time, and (2) the need to always have access to the ground-truth for each test example that gets explained. Re: (2), you do address this in Figure 4(b), but then seemingly do use ground-truth answers for the other RQ's. You also address (2) with RQ4, but again here the improvements seem very marginal and the section on this is very short, without baselines/ablations of the parts of the method used here (like utility). In summary, I feel like more of the paper should've been focused on these realistic setups where there is no access to ground-truth for each test example.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors investigate whether language models make good teachers for other, weaker language models. They do this through a systematic investigation of a teacher LM intervening at test time to improve a student LMs performance. They take seriously the idea that the teacher intervention imposes some cost (primarily on teacher, but also sometimes on the student) which then raises the question of whether teacher LMs can intervene efficiently. They dig deeper to see whether a teacher LM could personalise their explanations using a Theory of Mind based prompt, and whether teacher explanations generalize beyond the explained examples. Overall, they find positive evidence for all of these question, though with varying strengths, and introduce interesting new questions raised by this work.

STRENGTHS:
Overall, I thought this paper was highly original and refreshingly systematic, building a picture not only of Teacher LM capacities but generally how we should structure our own scientific thinking about teaching competencies in LMs. It was a pleasure to read, and I think will inspire new research in this domain.

WEAKNESSES:
I think the biggest weakness, which I see in too many ML evaluation papers, is that there doesn’t appear to be any statistical analysis of the results. Generally speaking, we don’t care about the particular set of prompts used, or these particular random seeds, but want to know if the results to the -- admittedly, well thought-out -- research questions generalize beyond the the details of these evaluations (eg., the random seeds used). These are well-designed studies with nice structure and control conditions, so it would make sense to construct multi-level (aka hierarchical or mixed-effects) logistic regression models predicting the correct choice as a function of intervention budget and teacher-student relationship (which varies across RQ). See See Lampinen et al. (2022) _Can language models learn from explanations in context?_ for an example of this kind of analysis in the language model evaluation context, or see Gelman and Hill (2006) for further background.

I found the language used to describe Theory of Mind was a little strong and oversold. It seems to me the authors take “having a Theory of Mind” and “having a Theory of Mind inspired prompt” to mean the same thing, when they are not. There are many papers in computational cognitive science that take seriously important features about Theory of Mind (e.g., that it is a “theory” and has certain structural properties) that would be valuable to engage with. Please engage with these and be more nuanced in your use of language around LLM capacities, especially around what it means to have a ToM
- Baker, C. L., Saxe, R., & Tenenbaum, J. B. (2009). Action understanding as inverse planning. Cognition, 113(3), 329-349.
- Jara-Ettinger, J., Gweon, H., Schulz, L. E., & Tenenbaum, J. B. (2016). The naïve utility calculus: Computational principles underlying commonsense psychology. Trends in cognitive sciences, 20(8), 589-604.
- Baker, C. L., Jara-Ettinger, J., Saxe, R., & Tenenbaum, J. B. (2017). Rational quantitative attribution of beliefs, desires and percepts in human mentalizing. Nature Human Behaviour, 1(4), 0064.

Also, there’s a difference between Theory of Mind and personalisation: One can have a ToM, but operate under bad assumptions about the other person’s mental state, and not be able to personalise very well. 

The authors talk about the trade-offs in teaching very nicely, and there is work in Cognitive Science concerning the same issues, and studying them in young children that I think the authors will find resonant.
- Bridgers, S., Jara-Ettinger, J., & Gweon, H. (2020). Young children consider the expected utility of others’ learning to decide what to teach. Nature human behaviour, 4(2), 144-152.

Some small points about Figure 1
- Might be easier to orient from Left to Right, with the Student on the left, for readability.
- A little bit confusing about what is an "answer" and what is an "explanation". In the text, it seems like the explanation is an actual informative explanation, but here it makes it seem like it's just the answer.

QUESTIONS:
- The authors mention a ‘human teacher’ in many of comparisons in the paper, but no mention of who or what this ‘human teacher’ is. Please clarify.
- The paper has several nicely consistent plots except for the y-axis, which makes it hard to really take advantage of the commonality of the designs and draw inferences. As well, scaling the y-axis on plots is generally bad practice as it can be misleading. Please unify the scale the y-axis.
- In the Single Round Intervention, is there a reason to not also have a setup where the student generates only a prediction without an explanation?
- The language at the end of the Single Round Intervention, talks about “When the teacher is a human, intervention happens with a human-written explanation.“, but in above it says that “e^i_H is always a human-written explanation”.
- On page 7, How does Expected Utility compare to True Utility? I may have missed something but does the teacher get the student confidence when it is not communicated? And if so, how?
- Also in the paragraph, the sentence “Ranking by teacher confidence is ineffective because it does not take the student into account.” reads like a tautology. Can you rephrase, or better yet, provide an example of how the teacher confidence can be misaligned from the student confidence?
- In the Teacher Theory of Mind Prompt, the teacher is steered towards explanations that help the student but not necessarily against explanations that do not help the student. I wonder if the authors have thought about if there’s a way of strengthening this manipulation by incorporating negative examples somehow.
- Under Baselines right under this, the word “rationalizations” appears when I think the authors mean “rationales”.
- The main results for RQ3 do not look like they’d be statistically significant with the appropriate statistical model. Please try to incorporate multi-level regression into your analysis so we can know where the evidence stands.
- I was little confused by the language in the intro to RQ4. The intuitive understanding of teacher explanations generalizing is showing a few explanations in the examples, and then having the student do their own CoT....Maybe this is what you’re saying, and the "that is" in the sentence tripped me up
- For the design of RQ4, Would this be the same as conditioning on the student explanations with (say) the correct answer?

LIMITATIONS:
I think the authors could talk more about the potential harms from excessive personalisation with LLMs.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
In this work, the authors tackle exciting research questions on how the teacher LLM intervention, which provides explanations in natural language, can enable the student LLM to make more accurate predictions. They specifically used StrategyQA, GSM8k, and CommonSenseQA for their experiments. The direction of the research is very important, and I liked the setup of formalizing the research questions with directly provided answers. However, the experimental design needs to be explained in more detail (possibly due to a missing appendix). I would also suggest considering the addition of significance testing for the obtained results to make them more convincing. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to include a discussion on how the results differ across different datasets and why.

Minor comments and questions:
- It would be helpful to explain the notation used in Fig 1 (e.g., in the caption or mention where the notation is introduced)
- '(details in Appendix A)' appendix is not available (but mentioned in the paper), so it's hard to judge some aspects of the paper since there are a lot of references to the discussion in the appendix
- 'mental model of the student' -> that needs some explanation
- The formulation of RQ1 sounds a bit ambiguous. Do the authors mean if the teacher intervention can help?
- RQ3: the notion of personalization is clearly explained (since it's an overloaded term, I would advise making it clear)
- RQ4 formulation can be clear
- 'Hence, the teacher communicates a partial explanation (specifically, only the first step of the rationale)' -- it would be great to show an example here
- lines 177-178, am I right that 1) are Teacher models, and 2) are models used for student
- Fig 2 :caption -> what is 'A + B = A student, B teacher'?
- lines 191-201: the study design is not clear from the perspectives of how a human teacher is introduced.  
- Fig 2: 'Flan-T5-XL + Flan-T5-Large' -- why the performance weakens withe more explanations?
- line 221 'communication cost' -- how would you measure communication cost?
- lines 224 - 255: How exactly confidence is estimated is unclear.
- fig 4 (a) why the accuracy goes down with more interventions.

STRENGTHS:
1. Exciting research direction that needs to be explored
2. Nice structure of the paper with RQ accompanied with answers.

WEAKNESSES:
1.The discussion in the paper appears fragmented, possibly due to the missing Appendix.
2. The experimental setup is not well-explained.
3. No significant testing has been applied.
4. It could be beneficial to include links to real-world applications that can benefit from this research.

QUESTIONS:
I mentioned them in the minor comments section above.

LIMITATIONS:
The authors addressed the limitations.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper studies how an LLM can be used to teach another LLM how to perform reasoning tasks by providing explanations (chain-of-thoughts). The paper starts by establishing that when the teacher LLM intervenes at test-time by providing such explanations, the student LLM accuracy is indeed improved compared to having the student rely on its own explanations (RQ1). Next, the paper considers the setup in which the intervention budget is limited (i.e., the teacher can provide explanations only for a limited number of test examples). The paper proposes a method (expected utility) for selecting which examples the teacher should provide explanations for (RQ2) and a method for generating personalized explanations for a particular student (RQ3). Both methods are based on prompting the teacher model with demonstrations of observed student predictions of historical examples. While RQ1, RQ2, and RQ3 study teacher intervention at test time, the paper lastly tests whether such interventions can be used by the student to perform better on test examples that the teacher has not provided explanations for (RQ4). The experiments are conducted on three tasks: StrategyQA, GSM8k, and CommonsenseQA with four publicly available LLMs: Flan-T5-Large, Flan-T5-XL, Llama-7B, and Llama-13B.

STRENGTHS:
1. The paper introduces and discusses interesting broad research questions that are likely to motivate future research efforts: (1) Evaluating LLMs from the perspective of teaching and improving weaker agents, (2) Creating personalized explanations for teaching, and (3) Distillation approaches that identify when a student lacks understanding.
	
2. The paper introduces an interesting method for estimating the utility of providing an explanation to the student model by simulating student behavior with different explanations. The paper demonstrates significant gains in results (Figure 3) and an interesting gap for improvement compared to the upper (True utility).

3. The paper proposes an innovative method for generating personalized explanations via conditioning on helpful (flip the incorrect prediction to a correct one) human explanations. The results are weak/mixed, though (please see #3 in Weaknesses).

WEAKNESSES:
1. In principle, providing explanations at test time for a set of test examples and achieving accuracy gains on those examples cannot be called "teaching". Teaching has to be evaluated by the ability of the taught model to generalize to new examples that it did not receive assistance with. However, the majority of this paper (RQ1, RQ2, and RQ3) defines teaching as providing explanations on test examples which seems quite misleading to me. 
	
2. RQ4 conduct proper evaluation for the teaching setup by measuring generalization to new examples (without teacher intervention). In that setup, the teacher intervenes in multiple rounds. At each round, the teacher chooses a few examples (two for FLAN-T5 and four for LLama) to provide teacher explanations for and use such examples as demonstrations for solving test examples. However,  the paper only compares to a zero-shot prompting baseline. It is not clear if the gain in accuracy is due to providing carefully chosen and personalized teacher explanations or it is just due to moving from zero-shot to few-shot. At least, the paper needs to compare to (1) providing unpersonalized teacher explanations for randomly chosen examples, and 2) few-shot prompting with generated student explanations for randomly chosen examples. Furthermore, the results in line 357-359 indicates that after the second round of intervention, the accuracy of the student drops/saturates. The paper does not provide any explanation for that behavior.

3. [Missing qualitative analysis of personalized explanations]. How different are they from the unpersonalized ones? The paper particularly needs to provide such analysis given that the results that support RQ3 are weak and somehow mixed (Figure 5 and Table 12 in the appendix): At 20% intervention, the gain is only 1.45% (maximum achieved gain) and at 80% intervention, unpersonalized explanations even yield better accuracy by 1.3%.

QUESTIONS:
1. Suggestion: One of the main aspects of this study is reducing communication cost between the teacher and the student. The paper needs to provide some real-world motivating scenarios for that requirement, and distinguish it clearly from the cost incurred by invoking the teacher model.
	
2. Suggestion: While establishing that CoT paradigm outperforms post-hoc rationales is interesting, including post-hoc rationales as a baseline in Figure 5 (RQ3) is not justified for the purpose of that experiment. For clarity, I suggest moving that baseline to a different part of the paper, remove it, or mention why it makes sense to include it in the context of RQ3. 
	
3. Minor typos - Table 8 & 9 in the appendix do not mention the task name. Appendices are referenced with letters A-E while the provided appendix is using section numbers.

LIMITATIONS:
After the discussions and new results provided by the authors (see below), I see two main limitations of this work:

1. While personalized explanations is a key aspect of the presented approach, end-to-end results show that it only leads to marginal gains. See the authors' comment below 'More baselines and analysis'

2. As discussed with the authors, the presentation of the experiments needs to be revised significantly to distinguish the evaluation of the components (RQ1, RQ2 and RQ3) from the end-to-end evaluation of the actual teaching scenario (RQ4) that the paper aims at. The experiments need to be a lot more focused on RQ4.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper investigates the potential of a Language Model (LLM) as a teacher for other, weaker LLMs by intervening at test time to improve the student LLM's performance. The authors focus on four main research questions (RQs): the effectiveness of teacher intervention, efficient intervention, personalization of explanations, and generalization of teacher explanations. The experiments are conducted using three tasks (StrategyQA, GSM8k, and CommonsenseQA) and four publicly available LLMs (Flan-T5-Large, Flan-T5-XL, Llama-7B, and Llama-13B). The study finds positive evidence for all research questions, but with varying strengths.

The paper proposes an interesting research direction and the experiments are generally well-designed and comprehensive. There are several valid concerns raised by the reviewers but the authors did a very good job addressing them during the discussion period. 
I agree with the reviewers that the  language used to describe the connection to Theory of Mind is not adequately supported by the paper. I urge the authors to take this and other comments strongly into consideration for improving the paper.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
